premise.title: Predicted Stability of fcc-YH10 at High Pressure

premises.premise: Theoretical calculations predict that the face-centred cubic (fcc) $YH_{10}$ phase becomes stable at a pressure of 250 GPa. [23]

Theoretical calculations suggest that while $YH_{10}$ may be unstable at 0 K, the difference in Gibbs free energies between the face-centred cubic (fcc) $YH_{10}$ phase and the hexagonal close packed (hcp) $YH_{9}$ phase decreases at higher temperatures, making $fcc-YH_{10}$ thermodynamically more favourable than $hcp-YH_{9}$ at temperatures above 1500 K at 375 GPa or above 1100 K at 400 GPa. [22]

In high-pressure synthesis experiments using ammonia borane ($NH_{3}BH_{3}$) as a hydrogen source, the presence of excess hydrogen ($H_2$) is confirmed if the initial yttrium trihydride ($YH_{3}$) phase completely transforms into the hydrogen-rich single-phase $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ after heating.

premise.title: Identification of Crystal Phases via Diffraction

premises.premise: The identification of specific yttrium hydride phases, such as $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$, is established by the agreement between experimental X-ray powder diffraction patterns and the calculated peak positions derived from theoretical crystal structures. [22]

reasoning.step: The authors note that theoretical predictions suggest the stability of a high-$T_{c}$ $fcc-YH_{10}$ phase at pressures above 250 GPa [@premise-1] or at high temperatures and pressures, specifically above 1100 K at 400 GPa [@premise-2].

Guided by these calculations, the authors performed experiments to synthesize yttrium hydrides using $YH_{3}$ and $NH_{3}BH_{3}$ at conditions covering the predicted stability range of $YH_{10}$, reaching pressures up to 410 GPa and temperatures up to 2250 K.
During pulsed laser heating of the samples at 410 GPa and temperatures up to 2250 K, the authors collected in situ X-ray diffraction data.

The authors analyzed the diffraction patterns and identified the formation of the $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ phase, observing agreement with calculated peak positions for this structure [@premise-4], while detecting only thermal expansion of the lattice.

The authors verified that the synthesis conditions allowed for the formation of the highest possible hydride by observing the complete transformation of the initial $YH_{3}$ into single-phase $YH_{9}$ [@premise-3], indicating that excess hydrogen was present.

Despite achieving conditions where $YH_{10}$ was predicted to be favorable [@premise-1, @premise-2] and ensuring excess hydrogen availability [@premise-3], the authors observed no diffraction peaks corresponding to the $fcc-YH_{10}$ phase in the final quenched products or during heating.

Based on the persistence of the $YH_{9}$ phase and the absence of $YH_{10}$ under these extreme conditions, the authors deduce that $P6_{3}/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ is the hydrogen-richest yttrium hydride stable in the studied range.

inference_unit.conclusion: Contrary to theoretical predictions suggesting the stability of a high-$T_{c}$ face-centered cubic (fcc) $YH_{10}$ phase, the authors conclude that $YH_{10}$ does not form in the pressure-temperature range up to 410 GPa and 2250 K. Instead, the authors establish that the $P6_3/mmc$ $YH_{9}$ phase remains the hydrogen-richest yttrium hydride stable under these conditions.
